Row1.Rationale: Atezolizumab blocks PD‑L1, preventing its engagement with PD‑1 and B7.1, reversing SHP‑mediated dephosphorylation of TCR/CD28 signaling components and restoring CD8⁺ T‑cell effector function in NSCLC and SCLC.

Row2.Rationale: Effective PD‑L1 blockade requires intact TCR recognition and CD28 co‑stimulation; when these are functional, released PD‑1 brake translates into clonal expansion, cytokine production, and tumor cell killing, underpinning benefit in inflamed NSCLC and SCLC‑I subtypes.

Row3.Rationale: CD8⁺ T cells activated by PD‑L1 blockade secrete IFN‑γ, which via JAK1/2–STAT1 upregulates PD‑L1 and MHC‑I; loss‑of‑function in IFNGR/JAK/STAT components abrogates this feedback, contributing to primary/acquired resistance to PD‑(L)1 inhibitors in lung cancer.

Row4.Rationale: High expression of HLA‑I and antigen‑processing genes enables presentation of neoantigens to CD8⁺ T cells; pan‑cancer and NSCLC data show that stronger MHC‑I pathway activity and CD8 infiltration correlate with higher response rates to PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade including atezolizumab.

Row5.Rationale: Elevated nonsynonymous mutation load increases neoantigen repertoire; in NSCLC, higher TMB associates with improved outcomes to atezolizumab‑based regimens, particularly when coupled to preserved MHC‑I and inflamed microenvironments.

Row6.Rationale: In IMpower150, adding bevacizumab to atezolizumab‑chemotherapy improved outcomes in NMF1 and NMF4 and mitigated resistance in NMF2, where macrophage/TGF‑β‑CAF–rich stroma and VEGF‑mediated exclusion of CD8⁺ T cells limited atezolizumab activity.

Row7.Rationale: Following PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade, NSCLC tumors can upregulate TIM‑3, LAG‑3 and other inhibitory receptors on T cells, sustaining exhaustion and limiting durability of atezolizumab responses despite continued PD‑L1 inhibition.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: Myeloid‑derived suppressor cells / Treg and immunosuppressive cytokines
Row8.Rationale: Enrichment of MDSCs, Tregs, and cytokines such as TGF‑β and certain chemokines in the NSCLC TME dampens effector T‑cell activity; these features are prominent in resistant transcriptomic subtypes (e.g., NMF2) with poor benefit from atezolizumab despite PD‑L1 expression.

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: Epigenetic repression of antigen presentation (e.g., LSD1‑mediated)
Row9.Rationale: In SCLC, overexpression of LSD1 and related epigenetic modifiers silences MHC‑I and interferon‑response genes, creating an “immune‑cold” state with limited benefit from PD‑L1 blockade; LSD1 inhibition can restore antigen presentation and sensitize tumors to anti‑PD‑L1 therapy.

Row10.Rationale: KRAS‑mutant NSCLC tends to show higher TMB/PD‑L1 and better ICI outcomes, whereas co‑mutations in STK11/KEAP1 drive metabolic reprogramming, low CD8 infiltration, and poor atezolizumab benefit, particularly in NMF2‑like phenotypes.

Row11.Rationale: The NMF4 subtype in IMpower150, enriched for B‑ and T‑cell–inflamed signatures, elevated PD‑L1, and favorable TMB, derived marked PFS/OS benefit from ABCP including atezolizumab, illustrating that pre‑existing adaptive immune activation strongly predicts sensitivity.

Row12.Rationale: In ES‑SCLC, a transcriptionally defined SCLC‑I subtype with higher CD8A/B and immune‑activation genes experiences greater survival benefit from EP‑atezolizumab in IMpower133, whereas non‑inflamed SCLC subtypes remain largely refractory.
